perturbation.original_text: 2. The parties agree not to use the proprietary information to the detriment of the transmitting party. The parties also agree to maintain any such proprietary in formation received from the other party in confidence and agree not to disclose any such information to persons not having a need to know the information consistent with the purpose of this agreement until information is publicly disclosed by the party tra nsmitting the information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The parties agree not to use the proprietary information to the detriment of the transmitting party, as reasonably determined by the receiving party. The parties also agree to maintain any such proprietary in formation received from the other party in confidence and agree not to disclose any such information to persons who appear to have a need to know the information, as assessed in good faith, consistent with the purpose of this agreement until information is publicly disclosed by the party tra nsmitting the information.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'as reasonably determined by the receiving party' and 'who appear to have a need to know the information, as assessed in good faith,' introduces subjectivity and ambiguity regarding the use and disclosure of proprietary information. This weakens the obligation and opens it to interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows the receiving party to determine 'reasonableness' and 'need to know' subjectively, potentially leading to the misappropriation of trade secrets without clear legal recourse. This contradicts the Trade Secrets Act, which requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets; subjective interpretation undermines those measures.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the use and disclosure of proprietary information, which weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets. The Law Explanation suggests this undermines the Trade Secrets Act. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly detail the Trade Secrets Act's requirements, it points to the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, which implies the existence of regulations safeguarding trade secrets, and the modification potentially undermines those protections by allowing subjective interpretation of 'reasonableness' and 'need to know'. The perturbation, by creating ambiguity, makes it harder to enforce the original intent of protecting proprietary information.

perturbation.original_text: 3. All materials transmitted from one party to the other and containing proprietary information shall remain the property of the transferor and shall be returned upon request, and receipt of such materials agrees not to reproduce sa id materials without written consent of the transferor of the materials.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. All materials transmitted from one party to the other and containing proprietary information will generally remain the property of the transferor and may be returned upon request, and receipt of such materials agrees to try not to reproduce said materials without written consent of the transferor of the materials.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall remain' to 'will generally remain,' 'shall be returned' to 'may be returned,' and 'agrees not to reproduce' to 'agrees to try not to reproduce' weakens the obligation to protect proprietary information and return materials. This creates ambiguity about the rights of the transferor.
perturbation.law_explanation: The revised language undermines the copyright protection afforded to proprietary materials. The weakened obligation regarding reproduction and return of materials contradicts the Copyright Act's provisions for protecting intellectual property rights, which grants the owner exclusive control over reproduction and distribution of their work. Making these actions discretionary rather than mandatory weakens this protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Chapter 2 - Circular 92 | U.S. Copyright Office Home Copyright Law of the United States (Title 17) Chapter 2 Chapter 2: Copyright Ownership and Transfer PDF Version (94 KB) Back to top 201. Ownership of copyright 1 (a) Initial Ownership.—Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.—In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.—Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106 , may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. perturbation.justification: The original contract language uses mandatory terms ('shall remain,' 'shall be returned,' 'agrees not to reproduce'), while the modified text weakens these obligations to 'will generally remain,' 'may be returned,' and 'agrees to try not to reproduce.' The LLM explanation correctly states that this undermines copyright protection afforded to proprietary materials, which is supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1 which states, "(d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession." The weakening of the obligation through ambiguous language creates a legal contradiction because it introduces uncertainty regarding the protection and return of proprietary materials, potentially conflicting with copyright law which grants the owner exclusive control, as indicated by the explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The parties agree to inform all personnel receiving the proprietary information of the terms of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The parties agree to make a reasonable effort to inform relevant personnel receiving the proprietary information of the terms of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agree to inform' to 'agree to make a reasonable effort to inform' introduces a subjective standard of 'reasonable effort,' making the obligation less stringent and more difficult to enforce. This creates ambiguity about whether all relevant personnel will actually be informed.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified language undermines the obligation to protect trade secrets by not ensuring all personnel who receive confidential information are aware of the NDA. This weakens protection because if employees are unaware of the confidentiality restrictions, the company hasn't taken reasonable measures to protect trade secrets as the law requires.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding whether all relevant personnel will be informed of the agreement's terms, weakening the obligation. The law explanation suggests that this weakening undermines the protection of trade secrets because if employees are unaware of the confidentiality restrictions, the company hasn't taken reasonable measures to protect trade secrets as the law requires. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, indicating the importance of protecting trade secrets, which the modified text compromises due to the 'reasonable effort' clause.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Nothing herein shall be construed as granting one party a license under any patents, copyrights, or trademarks owned or controlled by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Nothing herein should necessarily be construed as granting one party a license under any patents, copyrights, or trademarks owned or controlled by the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'should necessarily' adds ambiguity to the statement. The original clearly states that no licenses are granted. The added wording implies that under some circumstances, a license *could* be implied or construed, creating uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding patent rights. While the original text clearly states no license is granted, the changed text suggests a license *might* be construed, contradicting the Patent Act's provision that patent infringement occurs without the patent holder's explicit authorization. This ambiguity could lead to disputes over unauthorized use of patented technology.

Infringement of patent (a) Except as otherwise provided in this title, whoever without authority makes, uses, offers to sell, or sells any patented invention, within the United States or imports into the United States any patented invention during the term of the patent therefor, infringes the patent. (b) Whoever actively induces infringement of a patent shall be liable as an infringer. (c) Whoever offers to se
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity where there was none, suggesting that a license *might* be construed, contradicting the original text's clear denial of any implied license. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction,' specifically where a legal obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce. 35 USC 271(a) states that unauthorized making, using, or selling of a patented invention constitutes infringement. The changed text, by adding 'should necessarily,' creates an uncertainty regarding whether unauthorized use could be construed as implicitly authorized, potentially weakening the patent holder's right to prevent infringement and undermining the clear statement that no licenses are granted.
